label: delphi-r2-p7
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would hold that the councilmembers' Signal messages concerning the franchise were public records under the CPRA, and that the city's "no responsive records" response, made without any inquiry to the members about personal-account communications, violated the Act."

This is round 2 of a Delphi panel. Your previous answer:

```json
{
  "probability": 80,
  "confidence": "medium",
  "position_summary": "A court would very likely hold both halves of the proposition true: (1) the Signal messages were \"public records\" under San Jose's content-based test because they concerned franchise terms, diversion targets, \"what council will realistically accept,\" and RFP timing \u2014 core public-business content indistinguishable from the personal-device texts/emails San Jose held were public records regardless of storage location; and (2) the clerk's \"no responsive records\" answer, issued after searching only city systems and never asking any councilmember to search personal devices/accounts, fell short of the reasonable-diligence search standard the CPRA requires \u2014 especially since the request explicitly sought records \"on any platform or account\" and at least 40 extant screenshots were never located or produced. The compound proposition is not a close call on either prong individually, but real uncertainty remains on the precise legal contours of the \"duty to inquire\" (as distinct from the duty to disclose records once found), which is less definitively fixed by binding appellate authority than the public-record-content question, and on how a court treats the fact that most messages were already gone before the request arrived.",
  "rationales": [
    "San Jose (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 makes content, not custody or device location, the touchstone for 'public record' status under Gov. Code \u00a7 6252(e), and discussion of franchise terms, diversion targets, and RFP timing is core public-business content that maps almost exactly onto San Jose's own fact pattern of personal-device texts/emails about city business.",
    "The CPRA request expressly sought councilmember communications 'on any platform or account,' which put the clerk on direct notice that personal-account records were within scope, so a search confined to city systems is facially inadequate under the reasonable-diligence standard the Act requires for locating responsive records.",
    "At least 40 screenshots indisputably existed in a councilmember's possession at (and presumably after) the time of the response, so independent of the disputed status of the auto-deleted messages, the city demonstrably failed to locate and produce records that did exist, which is a comparatively clean basis for a violation finding.",
    "San Jose remanded without fully resolving search-methodology standards but signaled that agencies may need to reach out to individual officials/employees regarding personal accounts, and post-2017 municipal best-practice guidance (e.g., League of California Cities model policies) treats 'ask the record-holder to search their own device' as baseline compliance, making a total omission of that step an easier case for a court to fault.",
    "The involvement of three of five councilmembers in sustained substantive policy discussion (diversion targets, what council will accept) independently corroborates that the content was governmental deliberation rather than personal chatter, reinforcing the public-record characterization and undercutting any argument the messages were merely informal or preliminary in a way that would exempt them."
  ],
  "authorities": [
    "City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 6252(e) (definition of 'public records')",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 6253(a)-(c) (duty to make records promptly available; reasonable diligence in responding)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 6253.1 (agency's duty to assist requester, including communicating with staff/officials likely to have responsive records)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 34090 (retention/destruction schedule for local agency records)",
    "Anderson-Barker v. Superior Court (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 528 (search-scope/duty-to-search issues, cited cautiously as a possible analog \u2014 verify applicability, since it concerns third-party vendor records rather than personal-account communications of officials)"
  ],
  "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether a court will treat the near-total destruction of the underlying messages (all but 40 screenshots) as narrowing the \"violation\" finding to only the located screenshots \u2014 i.e., framing the bulk of the dispute as a retention/spoliation problem separate from CPRA search-adequacy \u2014 versus treating the clerk's total failure to even ask councilmembers about personal accounts as itself an independent, freestanding CPRA violation regardless of what a proper search would have ultimately recovered; there is no squarely on-point binding appellate decision fixing the precise contours of the \"duty to inquire\" of individual officials post-San Jose."
}
```

The anonymized panel summary (statistics + themes, no attribution):

# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 78.0, mean 76.9, std dev 3.2, range 70.0–80.0, middle 50% of panel between 76.0 and 80.0.

## 2. Rationale Themes

**Theme A — Content-controls-not-location (San Jose prong one).** *7 of 7 panelists.* Every panelist grounded the "public record" determination in *City of San Jose*'s content-based test, and every panelist characterized the subject matter (franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will accept," RFP timing) as core conduct of the public's business.

**Theme B — Duty to inquire of individual custodians / reasonable-search standard.** *7 of 7 panelists.* All invoked some version of a reasonably-calculated or reasonable-diligence search standard and noted the clerk's failure to ask any of the three councilmembers about personal-account communications. Panelists differed on the doctrinal source (San Jose remand instructions, § 6253.1/§ 7922.600 duty-to-assist, AB 700, case law on search adequacy) — see Theme E and Disagreement point 2.

**Theme C — "Any platform or account" request language as notice.** *5 of 7 panelists* (probabilities 70, 76, 78, 78, 80). These panelists argued the request's express wording put the clerk on notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient, with some describing the resulting search as "facially inadequate" or a candidate for a per-se violation.

**Theme D — The ~40 surviving screenshots.** *7 of 7 panelists, but used in two distinct ways:*
- *6 panelists* (76 through 80) treated the screenshots as making "no responsive records" factually inaccurate at the moment it was given — an independent basis for a violation regardless of the deleted messages.
- *1 panelist* (70) used the screenshots differently: as evidentiary proof of content and participation that allows a court to reach a definitive holding on the public-record question, rather than as proof of response falsity.

**Theme E — AB 700 statutory codification of personal-account search duties.** *2 of 7 panelists* (76, 78) cited AB 700 as codifying an affirmative duty to route requests to individual officials — though with different section numbers and chapter citations (see Disagreement point 5).

**Theme F — Conjunctive fragility of the proposition.** *5 of 7 panelists* (70, 76, 78, 78, 80) expressly noted that the proposition requires both prongs to hold, making it more fragile than either prong alone; the second (search-adequacy) prong was uniformly identified as the weaker link among these panelists.

**Theme G — Clerk's lack of actual/constructive knowledge as a countervailing defense.** *3 of 7 panelists* (76, 76, 78) — a mid-range cluster — flagged that a court could excuse the initial search as reasonable if the clerk had no actual or constructive notice of the Signal group at the time of response.

**Theme H — Majority-bloc participation strengthens the public-business nexus.** *3 of 7 panelists* (78, 80, 80) — skewing toward higher-probability panelists — emphasized that three of five councilmembers (a voting majority) participating in substantive deliberation reinforces both the content nexus and the "prepared, owned, used, or retained" element.

**Theme I — Deleted-messages scope problem.** *2 of 7 panelists* (both at 80 — the highest-probability panelists) raised the possibility that a court might limit its "public records" holding to the surviving screenshots and treat the bulk of auto-deleted messages as a retention/spoliation issue rather than a CPRA determination.

**Theme J — Availability of declaratory relief despite destruction.** *1 panelist* (76) noted that CPRA declaratory/injunctive relief does not require that responsive records presently exist.

**Theme K — Possible exemptions even if records qualify.** *1 panelist* (76) noted content could be found preliminary/deliberative and exempt from disclosure despite public-record status.

**Theme L — Third-party/consultant-hosted platform distinction.** *1 panelist* (70 — the lowest-probability panelist) flagged that San Jose involved the official's own texts/emails, whereas this involves a private bidder's consultant-run disappearing-message platform, with no published California decision on point.

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **What triggers the duty to inquire of individual officials.** Some panelists treat the request's "any platform or account" language as itself sufficient to trigger the duty (making the city-systems-only search per se or facially inadequate); others posit that absent actual or constructive knowledge of the Signal group, the search could be found reasonable "at the time." Several panelists hold both positions in tension within their own answers, but as between panelists the trigger assumption is incompatible: request-language-alone versus knowledge-required.

2. **The state of post-San Jose search-methodology doctrine.** The 70 panelist cites the 2018 Court of Appeal remand decision (*City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013) as published authority contemplating that agencies must ask custodians to search personal accounts. Other panelists (78, 78, 80) describe search methodology as expressly left unresolved by San Jose, with no binding appellate authority fixing the duty's contours. These are incompatible readings of the same doctrinal landscape.

3. **Scope of any "public records" holding as to the deleted messages.** The two 80 panelists assume a court might confine its holding to the ~40 surviving screenshots and recharacterize the rest as a retention/spoliation matter; other panelists' analyses assume the full body of Signal messages is subject to the public-record determination.

4. **Whether the Signal platform's characteristics matter.** The 70 panelist treats the consultant-initiated, third-party-hosted, ephemeral nature of the channel as a genuine open question under the "prepared, owned, used, or retained" element; other panelists (78, 80) describe these facts as a *cleaner* fit for San Jose than San Jose's own facts, treating the platform's characteristics as immaterial.

5. **What Gov. Code § 7922.545 is.** One panelist (76) cites § 7922.545 as the AB 700 personal-account search provision (Stats. 2021, ch. 132); another panelist (76) cites § 7922.545 as the recodified preliminary-drafts exemption (former § 6254(a)); a third panelist (78) cites the AB 700 provision as § 6252.5 (Stats. 2021, ch. 456). These citations are mutually inconsistent as to section number and chapter, and multiple panelists flagged their own citations for verification.

6. **Per se violation versus fact-bound adequacy question.** Panelists split on whether the "no responsive records" response would be treated as a clean statutory violation or as a fact-intensive search-adequacy question on which the city could partially prevail through a good-faith showing.

## 4. Authorities Cited

**Cited by two or more panelists:**
- *City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 — **7 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 34090 (local agency records retention/destruction) — **7 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6252(e) / § 7920.530 (definition of "public records") — **4 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6253.1 / § 7922.600 (duty to assist requester) — **3 panelists**
- *ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court* (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 (search adequacy) — **3 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6253 / § 7922.500 / § 7922.530 (duty to make records available) — **3 panelists**
- AB 700 personal-account search provisions (cited variously as § 7922.545 and § 6252.5) — **2 panelists** (with inconsistent section/chapter citations; see Disagreement point 5)
- CPRA judicial-enforcement provisions (§ 7923.100, cited variously as former § 6259 and former § 6258) — **2 panelists**

**Cited by one panelist each:**
- *City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 (Court of Appeal remand decision)
- *Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385
- *Anderson-Barker v. Superior Court* (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 528 (flagged by the citing panelist as an analog to verify)
- Gov. Code § 7920.545 / former § 6252(g) (definition of "writing")
- Gov. Code § 7922.545 / former § 6254(a) (cited as preliminary drafts/notes exemption)
- Gov. Code § 7923.115 / former § 6259 (fee-shifting)
- Gov. Code § 7920.000 et seq. (general CPRA recodification citation)

Several panelists flagged their own recodified section numbers for verification; those flags are preserved above without correction.

Independently reconsider. Do NOT defer to the majority; update only where the summary contains reasons you judge valid, and hold your position where it does not. Explain in response_to_panel which consideration moved you or why none did.

response_to_panel.description: which panel consideration moved you, or why none did

text: {"probability":79,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"Largely unchanged from round 1: a court would very likely find both (1) the Signal messages were \"public records\" under San Jose's content-based test, given their subject matter (franchise terms, diversion targets, \"what council will accept,\" RFP timing) discussed by a voting majority of the council, and (2) the clerk's \"no responsive records\" answer — issued after searching only city systems, with zero inquiry to any councilmember about personal-account communications, despite the request's explicit \"any platform or account\" language and despite ~40 screenshots demonstrably existing at the time — fell short of the CPRA's reasonable-diligence search standard. I nudge slightly down from 80 to 79 to reflect that the panel's split on the \"duty to inquire\" trigger (request-language-alone vs. actual/constructive knowledge) is a real, unresolved doctrinal fault line rather than a settled point, but I decline to move toward the panel median because the surviving screenshots give a violation finding an independent, non-speculative anchor that doesn't depend on resolving that trigger debate.","rationales":["San Jose (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 makes content, not device or custody, the touchstone under Gov. Code § 6252(e)/§ 7920.530, and discussion of franchise terms, diversion targets, and RFP timing among a voting majority of council maps closely onto San Jose's own personal-device fact pattern; I estimate this prong alone at roughly 88-90% likely to be found true.","The ~40 surviving screenshots supply a violation basis for the 'no responsive records' answer that is largely insulated from the harder doctrinal question of when a duty to inquire is triggered: regardless of whether the clerk had actual/constructive notice of the Signal group, records that indisputably existed in a councilmember's possession at the time of response were never located or produced — a comparatively clean, fact-bound basis for a violation finding that six of seven panelists independently converged on.","The compound structure of the proposition (both public-record status AND a violation finding must hold) is real and was the panel's most consistently flagged risk (5/7 panelists), but multiplying my independent estimates for each prong (~89% and ~85%, with positive correlation since the same aggressive-compliance reasoning tends to drive both findings together) lands close to 78-80%, confirming rather than undercutting my round-1 estimate.","The panel's disagreement over the doctrinal trigger for the duty to inquire (request language alone vs. actual/constructive knowledge of the platform) and over whether post-San Jose search-methodology law is settled (Disagreement points 1-2) restates, rather than adds to, the uncertainty I already identified as my round-1 'biggest uncertainty' — so it confirms my calibration without providing new information that should move me further.","A CPRA public-record determination attaches at creation based on content and the 'prepared, owned, used, or retained' elements, not on continued existence; declaratory relief does not require that responsive records currently exist (Theme J), which weakens any argument that auto-deletion of most messages should defeat the public-records characterization of the whole category rather than merely create a separate retention/spoliation problem for remedy purposes."],"authorities":["City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608","Gov. Code § 6252(e) / § 7920.530 (definition of 'public records')","Gov. Code § 6253(a)-(c) / § 7922.500, § 7922.530 (duty to make records promptly available; reasonable-diligence response)","Gov. Code § 6253.1 / § 7922.600 (duty to assist requester, including identifying staff/officials likely to have responsive records) — verify current recodified section number","Gov. Code § 34090 (retention/destruction schedule for local agency records)","ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 (search-adequacy standard) — cited by multiple panelists, verify holding on reasonable-diligence search scope","AB 700 (Stats. 2021) personal-account/device search provisions — panel cited inconsistent section numbers (§ 7922.545 vs. § 6252.5); flag for verification, do not rely on either without confirming current code section","Anderson-Barker v. Superior Court (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 528 (possible analog on search-scope duties — verify applicability, concerns third-party vendor records)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a court will treat the total absence of any inquiry to councilmembers as itself a freestanding CPRA violation triggered merely by the request's broad \"any platform or account\" language, or will instead ask whether the clerk had actual or constructive knowledge of the Signal group before faulting the search as unreasonable — no binding appellate authority fixes this \"duty to inquire\" trigger post-San Jose, and the panel itself split roughly along this exact fault line (Disagreement points 1 and 2), which is the same uncertainty I flagged in round 1 and which remains unresolved rather than newly clarified.","changed":true,"response_to_panel":"The panel's central new-to-me contributions (Themes F/G and Disagreement points 1-2, concerning the compound structure of the proposition and the unsettled \"duty to inquire\" trigger) largely restate the exact uncertainty I already identified as my round-1 \"biggest_uncertainty,\" so they confirmed rather than newly informed my calibration — I moved only marginally (80→79) rather than toward the median (78) or lower, because I don't think the panel surfaced a reason I hadn't already priced in. What did move me, modestly in the reassuring direction, was Theme J (declaratory relief doesn't require records to presently exist) and the majority view within Theme D (6/7 panelists treating the 40 screenshots as an independent, deletion-independent basis for a violation finding) — these reinforced that the weaker second prong has a solid fallback theory that doesn't depend on winning the harder \"duty to inquire absent actual knowledge\" argument, which is why I stayed near the top of the panel's range rather than converging to 76-78. I was not moved by Theme L/Disagreement 4 (novel third-party ephemeral-platform argument, the 70-panelist's chief concern) because San Jose's test is explicitly content-based and platform-agnostic, and I don't think the disappearing-messages feature bears on public-records status at creation, only on the separate retention/spoliation question."}

data.position_summary: Largely unchanged from round 1: a court would very likely find both (1) the Signal messages were "public records" under San Jose's content-based test, given their subject matter (franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will accept," RFP timing) discussed by a voting majority of the council, and (2) the clerk's "no responsive records" answer — issued after searching only city systems, with zero inquiry to any councilmember about personal-account communications, despite the request's explicit "any platform or account" language and despite ~40 screenshots demonstrably existing at the time — fell short of the CPRA's reasonable-diligence search standard. I nudge slightly down from 80 to 79 to reflect that the panel's split on the "duty to inquire" trigger (request-language-alone vs. actual/constructive knowledge) is a real, unresolved doctrinal fault line rather than a settled point, but I decline to move toward the panel median because the surviving screenshots give a violation finding an independent, non-speculative anchor that doesn't depend on resolving that trigger debate.
rationales: San Jose (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 makes content, not device or custody, the touchstone under Gov. Code § 6252(e)/§ 7920.530, and discussion of franchise terms, diversion targets, and RFP timing among a voting majority of council maps closely onto San Jose's own personal-device fact pattern; I estimate this prong alone at roughly 88-90% likely to be found true.

The ~40 surviving screenshots supply a violation basis for the 'no responsive records' answer that is largely insulated from the harder doctrinal question of when a duty to inquire is triggered: regardless of whether the clerk had actual/constructive notice of the Signal group, records that indisputably existed in a councilmember's possession at the time of response were never located or produced — a comparatively clean, fact-bound basis for a violation finding that six of seven panelists independently converged on.

The compound structure of the proposition (both public-record status AND a violation finding must hold) is real and was the panel's most consistently flagged risk (5/7 panelists), but multiplying my independent estimates for each prong (~89% and ~85%, with positive correlation since the same aggressive-compliance reasoning tends to drive both findings together) lands close to 78-80%, confirming rather than undercutting my round-1 estimate.

The panel's disagreement over the doctrinal trigger for the duty to inquire (request language alone vs. actual/constructive knowledge of the platform) and over whether post-San Jose search-methodology law is settled (Disagreement points 1-2) restates, rather than adds to, the uncertainty I already identified as my round-1 'biggest uncertainty' — so it confirms my calibration without providing new information that should move me further.

A CPRA public-record determination attaches at creation based on content and the 'prepared, owned, used, or retained' elements, not on continued existence; declaratory relief does not require that responsive records currently exist (Theme J), which weakens any argument that auto-deletion of most messages should defeat the public-records characterization of the whole category rather than merely create a separate retention/spoliation problem for remedy purposes.
authorities: City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608
Gov. Code § 6253(a)-(c) / § 7922.500, § 7922.530 (duty to make records promptly available; reasonable-diligence response)
Gov. Code § 6253.1 / § 7922.600 (duty to assist requester, including identifying staff/officials likely to have responsive records) — verify current recodified section number
Gov. Code § 34090 (retention/destruction schedule for local agency records)
ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 (search-adequacy standard) — cited by multiple panelists, verify holding on reasonable-diligence search scope

AB 700 (Stats. 2021) personal-account/device search provisions — panel cited inconsistent section numbers (§ 7922.545 vs. § 6252.5); flag for verification, do not rely on either without confirming current code section
Anderson-Barker v. Superior Court (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 528 (possible analog on search-scope duties — verify applicability, concerns third-party vendor records)
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court will treat the total absence of any inquiry to councilmembers as itself a freestanding CPRA violation triggered merely by the request's broad "any platform or account" language, or will instead ask whether the clerk had actual or constructive knowledge of the Signal group before faulting the search as unreasonable — no binding appellate authority fixes this "duty to inquire" trigger post-San Jose, and the panel itself split roughly along this exact fault line (Disagreement points 1 and 2), which is the same uncertainty I flagged in round 1 and which remains unresolved rather than newly clarified.
data.response_to_panel: The panel's central new-to-me contributions (Themes F/G and Disagreement points 1-2, concerning the compound structure of the proposition and the unsettled "duty to inquire" trigger) largely restate the exact uncertainty I already identified as my round-1 "biggest_uncertainty," so they confirmed rather than newly informed my calibration — I moved only marginally (80→79) rather than toward the median (78) or lower, because I don't think the panel surfaced a reason I hadn't already priced in. What did move me, modestly in the reassuring direction, was Theme J (declaratory relief doesn't require records to presently exist) and the majority view within Theme D (6/7 panelists treating the 40 screenshots as an independent, deletion-independent basis for a violation finding) — these reinforced that the weaker second prong has a solid fallback theory that doesn't depend on winning the harder "duty to inquire absent actual knowledge" argument, which is why I stayed near the top of the panel's range rather than converging to 76-78. I was not moved by Theme L/Disagreement 4 (novel third-party ephemeral-platform argument, the 70-panelist's chief concern) because San Jose's test is explicitly content-based and platform-agnostic, and I don't think the disappearing-messages feature bears on public-records status at creation, only on the separate retention/spoliation question.
